# **High-Resolution Paleo-Coastline Data and Integration Strategies for Palaeoenvironmental Reconstruction**

## **Introduction and Architectural Context**

The reconstruction of paleogeographic environments—particularly the precise delineation of paleo-coastlines—is a foundational requirement for earth system modeling, archaeological mapping, and paleoclimate downscaling. The current architecture of the Paleo: Tools Mapper suite relies on the ICE-6G\_C (VM5a) model, utilizing a ![][image1] NetCDF grid to serve relative sea level and topographic anomalies from 26 ka to the present at 0.5 ka intervals. The application pipeline involves a Python FastAPI backend that parses these NetCDF grids, generates a GeoJSON FeatureCollection, and serves it to a MapLibre GL frontend via the GET /paleo-coastline?time\_ka=X endpoint contract.  
While the ICE-6G\_C model represents a highly robust, peer-reviewed global glacial isostatic adjustment (GIA) framework, its baseline spatial resolution of 1° poses severe limitations for interactive, high-fidelity web mapping. At the equator, a 1° grid cell spans approximately 111 kilometers. This fundamental spatial constraint forces the rendering engine to generate highly digitized, "blocky" coastal margins. Even when standard vector smoothing algorithms are applied post-extraction, the sheer distance between vertices fails to capture critical local geomorphological features, such as paleo-estuaries, submerged peninsulas, complex archipelago structures, and continental shelf break dynamics. The transition from 1° gridded data to sub-degree resolution requires a comprehensive reassessment of the entire data pipeline.  
This report provides an exhaustive evaluation of sub-degree paleo-topography models, plate-tectonic reconstruction APIs, pre-computed spatial datasets, and modern geospatial engineering practices. The objective is to identify a data source and integration strategy that optimizes the inherent trade-offs between spatial resolution, data volume, and the computational efficiency of the application's GeoJSON delivery system. By examining the geophysical underpinnings of various elevation models and the algorithmic mechanisms of coastline extraction, this document outlines a definitive roadmap for upgrading the Paleo Mapper tool to achieve smooth, high-resolution vector coastlines.

## **High-Resolution Paleo-Topography Data Sources**

To resolve the resolution bottleneck, it is necessary to evaluate the available lineage of global GIA models, paleodigital elevation models (PaleoDEMs), and the specific geophysical assumptions that govern their topographic outputs. The evaluation encompasses the ICE-6G\_C high-resolution variants, legacy 10-minute models, advanced deep-time datasets, and cutting-edge topobathymetric downscaling techniques.

### **The ICE-6G\_C O512 Model: Resolution and Access**

The ICE-6G\_C (VM5a) model is widely recognized as a robust framework for mapping ice-age terminal deglaciation. The model is intricately constrained by space geodesy, global relative sea-level (RSL) histories, and exposure age dating, offering an unparalleled reconstruction of eustatic sea-level equivalent dynamics.1 While the standard distribution utilized by the Paleo Mapper suite operates on a 1° grid (calculated at spherical harmonic degree and order 256), a higher-resolution variant of the model exists and is actively maintained.  
The ICE-6G\_C model calculations have been executed at degree and order 512 (O512). In spectral modeling, doubling the maximum degree and order effectively halves the spatial wavelength of the resolved features. Consequently, the O512 variant features a full resolution of approximately 40 kilometers.1 This higher spatial frequency is particularly significant in regions with narrow geographical and glaciological constraints, such as the Antarctic Peninsula, where the distinction between grounded ice, floating ice shelves, and open ocean requires finer gradients.5 When compared to satellite altimetry data such as CryoSat-2, the uncertainties in ice mass distribution for the O512 model are drastically reduced, typically by more than half, across almost all glacial basins.1  
Access to the ICE-6G\_C O512 dataset is centralized on the primary data repository maintained by W.R. Peltier at the University of Toronto. The files can be accessed directly at the verified URL: https://www.atmosp.physics.utoronto.ca/\~peltier/data.php.5 The files are hosted in NetCDF format, identical to the 1° versions, and can be identified by the "O512" string embedded within the filename.5 While upgrading to the O512 model offers a nearly threefold improvement in linear resolution (reducing the grid cell size from 111 km to 40 km), the output will likely remain insufficiently detailed for hyper-local coastal mapping in MapLibre GL. A 40 km coastal vertex spacing still heavily abstracts the geometry of intricate coastal zones like the Baltic Sea or the Indonesian archipelago.

### **Evaluation of ICE-5G v1.2 (10-minute resolution)**

Given the limitations of a 40 km grid, attention naturally shifts to datasets boasting sub-degree resolutions. The earlier ICE-5G (VM2) v1.2 model is notably available at a 10-minute (10') spatial resolution.6 A 10-minute grid translates to a spatial resolution of roughly 18.5 kilometers (or ![][image2]) at the equator, representing a drastic geometric improvement over both the 1° and O512 grids. Historically, the 10-minute ICE-5G dataset was pivotal in modeling the Last Glacial Maximum (LGM) and was heavily utilized by the oceanographic community to force paleotidal simulations, evaluate tidal range changes, and drive high-resolution paleoclimate general circulation models.7  
However, relying on ICE-5G to achieve higher resolution is geophysically retrograde and introduces significant errors in relative sea-level prediction. The ICE-5G model utilizes the VM2 radial viscosity profile.6 In the VM2 structure, the lithosphere is modeled as an entirely elastic layer that is 90 km thick.6 In contrast, the updated VM5a profile used by ICE-6G\_C models the lithosphere as an elastic layer 60 km thick situated above a highly viscous (![][image3] Pa s) layer that is 40 km thick.10 This structural difference profoundly alters the mechanics of forebulge collapse—the subsidence of the peripheral mantle bulge that forms around the margins of massive ice sheets.  
Applying the obsolete VM2 profile results in marked discrepancies when attempting to reconcile predicted relative sea-level histories with geological constraints, particularly along the eastern coast of North America and in regions subject to complex rotational feedback.11 Furthermore, the ICE-5G ice thickness history predicts vastly different regional glaciation patterns compared to the modern consensus, such as divergent uplift centers over the Keewatin sector of the Canadian Shield.7 Therefore, while ICE-5G offers the highly desirable 10' resolution, its geophysical inaccuracies render it an unsuitable choice for a state-of-the-art palaeoenvironmental reconstruction suite.

### **The Superior Alternative: ICE-7G\_NA (VM7)**

The limitations of the ICE-6G O512 resolution and the obsolescence of ICE-5G 10' are fully resolved by the introduction of the state-of-the-art ICE-7G\_NA (VM7) model. This model represents the most extensively validated combination of ice thickness history and radial viscosity profile currently available from the Peltier lineage.5 The VM7 viscosity profile refines the stiffness of the upper mantle, which strongly impacts the amplitude of observed uplift and subsidence, and carefully accounts for the local sensitivities of relative sea-level histories that ICE-6G failed to perfectly reconcile.5  
Crucially for the Paleo Mapper tool, the topography, bathymetry, and ice thickness outputs of ICE-7G\_NA are published in multiple resolutions, including a highly advantageous 10-minute (10') grid.5 Hosted at the same verified University of Toronto repository (https://www.atmosp.physics.utoronto.ca/\~peltier/data.php), the dataset spans the requested 0–26 ka window at the precise 0.5 ka intervals required by the current API contract.5  
The transition to ICE-7G\_NA 10' offers an optimal balance between spatial fidelity and temporal relevance. The 10-minute grid cells (![][image4] km) allow for the extraction of substantially smoother and more realistic coastal polygons. The 10-minute ICE-7G\_NA NetCDF files for individual time slices (e.g., 21 KBP) are approximately 27 MB in size, compared to the 773 KB size of their 1° counterparts.5 This manageable increase in data volume makes ICE-7G\_NA 10' the most geophysically accurate and structurally suitable drop-in replacement for Quaternary coastal rendering.

### **Scotese & Wright (2018) PaleoDEMs: Scope and Sea-Level Adjustment**

For applications extending beyond the Late Quaternary period, the PALEOMAP PaleoDEMs developed by Scotese and Wright (2018) provide an exhaustive digital representation of paleotopography and paleobathymetry spanning the entire Phanerozoic eon (540 Ma to 0 Ma).15 While traditionally distributed at a ![][image1] resolution in CSV and NetCDF formats 16, the EarthByte research group has published an ultra-high-resolution version of these PaleoDEMs at a ![][image5] (6-minute) resolution.17 This 0.1° grid translates to an exceptional 11.1 km resolution at the equator. These high-resolution files are hosted exclusively as NetCDF files on the Zenodo repository under the verified DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.5460860.15  
The Scotese PaleoDEMs account for the shifting distribution of deep ocean basins, shallow epicontinental seas, lowlands, and mountainous regions across deep geological time.16 Addressing a specific research question regarding sea-level adjustment: the Scotese datasets do incorporate explicit sea-level constraints. The coastlines associated with these topographies have been carefully adjusted to represent maximum transgressive surfaces.18 This adjustment is empirically grounded, indicated and validated by stage-level marine fossil occurrences extracted from the Paleobiology Database (PBDB).18  
Despite the unparalleled ![][image6] spatial resolution and the rigorous sea-level adjustments, the Scotese PaleoDEMs present a critical temporal limitation for the Paleo Mapper's specific use case. The dataset is structured in 5-million-year (5 Myr) intervals.16 This temporal granularity is designed to capture slow tectonic drift, supercontinent formation, and long-term greenhouse/icehouse climatic shifts. It completely fails to capture the rapid, millennial-scale eustatic sea-level fluctuations driven by the growth and decay of Quaternary ice sheets. If the Paleo Mapper tool strictly targets the 0–26 ka window at 0.5 ka intervals, the Scotese dataset cannot provide the required temporal dynamics. It is, however, the definitive and recommended data source if the application's scope expands to include deep-time tectonic reconstructions.

| Dataset Model | Spatial Resolution | Equator Distance | Temporal Range | Temporal Interval | Best Use Case |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| ICE-6G\_C (VM5a) | 1° (Degree 256\) | \~111 km | 0–122 ka | 0.5 ka | Legacy baseline mapping |
| ICE-6G\_C O512 | \~0.36° (Degree 512\) | \~40 km | 0–122 ka | 0.5 ka | Regional ice mass modeling |
| ICE-5G (VM2) | 10' (\~0.166°) | \~18.5 km | 0–21 ka | 0.5 / 1.0 ka | Obsolete; paleotidal baselines |
| ICE-7G\_NA (VM7) | 10' (\~0.166°) | \~18.5 km | 0–26 ka | 0.5 ka | **Optimal Quaternary Coastlines** |
| Scotese PaleoDEM | 6' (0.1°) | \~11.1 km | 0–540 Ma | 5.0 Myr | Deep-time Phanerozoic mapping |

### **Advanced Continental Pipelines: The Anomaly (Delta) Method**

If a 10-minute resolution still proves too coarse for highly localized archaeological or paleohydrological applications, the most advanced approach to paleo-coastline generation abandons raw GIA outputs in favor of dynamic downscaling. This technique, prevalent in cutting-edge paleoclimate modeling, is formally known as the "anomaly method" or "delta method".21  
The underlying physical principle acknowledges that modern digital elevation models—such as the 15-arc-second ETOPO 2022 global relief model, the GEBCO 2023 grid, or the 30-meter FABDEM—contain highly accurate, fine-scale geomorphic details that are mathematically absent from computationally expensive GIA models.24 ETOPO 2022, for instance, provides a continuous, global terrain model for ocean and land at a spatial resolution of 15 arc-seconds (roughly 450 meters), integrating satellite altimetry lidar and sonar soundings.24  
The anomaly method calculates the numerical difference (the anomaly or "delta") between the GIA model's present-day prediction and its past prediction (e.g., at 12 ka).21 This low-resolution anomaly field represents the pure glacial isostatic deformation and eustatic sea-level change. The low-resolution anomaly is then bilinearly interpolated and subtracted from the high-resolution modern observed topography.21 This method preserves the full 15-arc-second (or 30-meter) detail of the modern surface while accurately displacing that surface vertically according to the ICE-7G loading history.21  
Open-source pipelines, such as PaleoEurope-DEM v1.0, have successfully deployed this exact methodology using a distributed processing pipeline (combining FABDEM, GEBCO, and ICE-6G VM5a) to yield seamless coastal transitions at a 30-meter horizontal resolution.21 While dynamically calculating these arrays via the FastAPI backend would introduce insurmountable latency, executing the delta method as a pre-computation step allows for the generation of the highest possible resolution coastal vectors without sacrificing the geophysical accuracy of the Peltier models.

## **The GPlates Web Service (GWS) Architecture**

For dynamic coastline reconstruction, particularly when evaluating tectonic drift alongside sea-level change, the EarthByte group has developed the GPlates Web Service (gws.gplates.org). This service provides a robust, HTTP-accessible REST API that exposes the powerful PyGPlates reconstruction engine to web applications.29 Evaluating the integration of GWS requires an understanding of its endpoints, data quality constraints, pricing models, and deployment architectures.

### **Coastline Resolution and Smoothness Mechanics**

The GWS features a specific, dedicated endpoint for coastline reconstruction: GET /reconstruct/coastlines/. This endpoint returns standard reconstructed features in GeoJSON format, which can be directly parsed by a MapLibre GL frontend.30 The service relies on defined tectonic plate models specified via URL parameters (e.g., model=ZAHIROVIC2022, model=MULLER2019, model=CAO2024).32 If no model is specified, the service defaults to ZAHIROVIC2022.32  
Regarding the critical question of whether the service returns "smooth vector coastlines," it is vital to understand the internal mechanics of the PyGPlates engine. The GWS is fundamentally a *rotation* engine, not a dynamic sea-level calculator. It takes pre-existing static polygons, continental fragments, or coastline vectors that are hard-coded into the specified tectonic model and applies Euler pole rotations to calculate their paleo-coordinates for a given time.33  
Consequently, the GWS does *not* natively compute eustatic sea-level adjustments or generate high-resolution topographically derived coastlines on the fly based on gridded DEMs.31 The smoothness of the returned vector coastlines is entirely and exclusively dependent on the spatial resolution of the static coastline shapefiles embedded within the requested model.30 If the underlying model contains highly detailed, densely packed vector nodes, the GWS output will be perfectly smooth. If the model relies on generalized, simplified geometries for computational speed during continental drift simulations, the output will remain angular and coarse. Furthermore, these tectonic models primarily address lithospheric drift over millions of years; they are not inherently designed to track the rapid, topography-driven coastline shifts caused by 120-meter sea-level drops during the Last Glacial Maximum.

### **Service Access, Rate Limits, and Pricing**

The primary public GPlates Web Service is hosted by the National Computational Infrastructure (NCI) under the AuScope scheme, and the software is maintained by the EarthByte research group at the University of Sydney.29 The service is entirely free to the scientific, educational, and public community.29  
Extensive documentation and source code reviews indicate that there are no API keys required for general access to the reconstruction endpoints.29 While hard rate limits for the coastline reconstruction endpoint are not officially published in the user documentation, parameters exist to limit data payloads (such as the limit={NUMBER\_OF\_RECORDS} parameter utilized in the paleobiology database query examples).35 However, relying on a public, shared academic server for a production-grade FastAPI frontend introduces significant risks regarding network latency, concurrent connection throttling, and unpredictable server downtime.36

### **Self-Hosting with Docker**

To mitigate latency and prevent potential throttling during high-volume requests from the Paleo Mapper frontend, self-hosting the GWS is highly recommended and actively supported by the EarthByte group.29 A lightweight, production-ready Docker image is available directly from Docker Hub under the repository gplates/gws.36  
Deploying the service locally, on a dedicated VPS, or within a Kubernetes cluster can be achieved with a simple, one-line execution command: docker run \-d \--rm \-p 18000:80 gplates/gws.36  
For advanced production environments, the official GitHub repository provides a comprehensive docker-compose setup. This orchestration natively links the GWS Python application with a dedicated Redis cache and a local GPlates spatial database volume, vastly accelerating spatial queries and raster lookups.37 Self-hosting eliminates internet routing latency, guarantees continuous service availability, and, most importantly, allows developers to mount their own custom, ultra-high-resolution static coastline polygons into the local database instance for rotation.36

| GWS Feature | Specification / Detail |
| :---- | :---- |
| Endpoint | https://gws.gplates.org/reconstruct/coastlines/ |
| Parameters | time (Ma), model (String), fmt (json/png) |
| Output Format | GeoJSON FeatureCollection (default) |
| Cost / API Key | Free / No API Key required |
| Smoothness | Model-dependent (rotates static vectors) |
| Self-Hosting | Fully supported via gplates/gws Docker image |

## **Pre-Computed Coastline Datasets and Web Tile Services**

If computing coastline contours programmatically from NetCDF grids proves too resource-intensive, and if the GPlates Web Service models lack the necessary Quaternary eustatic detail, integrating pre-computed, peer-reviewed spatial datasets is a highly viable alternative.

### **Readily Available Vector Coastlines**

Several high-quality, pre-computed vector datasets focus on specific geological epochs, providing ready-to-use geometries.

1. **Scotese PaleoAtlas:** Complementing the PaleoDEMs, ESRI shapefiles of continental margins and paleocoastlines have been generated for the entire Phanerozoic in 5-million-year intervals.18 These coastlines represent maximum transgressive surfaces and are available as version v7.18 The dataset is hosted on Zenodo (DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.4017820).18 Because they are standard ESRI shapefiles, they are natively compatible with GPlates for rotation and can be easily converted to GeoJSON using standard GIS tools like GDAL/OGR.18  
2. **CRC806 Paleocoastlines GIS Dataset:** This dataset explicitly targets the Late Quaternary, providing 23 different modeled land masks of characteristic sea-level high stands and low stands from 140 ka to the present.38 Generated using the GEBCO 2014 bathymetric DEM combined with sea-level data extracted from global geoarchives, the dataset includes polygons representing highly accurate coastlines (e.g., the \-120m isobath for the Weichselian/LGM period, and the 6 ka Mediterranean shorelines) across Europe and Northern Africa.38  
3. **World Atlas of Last Interglacial Shorelines (WALIS):** Focused entirely on Marine Isotope Stage 5e (![][image7] to 130 ka), this standardized database contains thousands of sea-level indicators published since the early 1900s.41 While largely comprised of point-based proxy data rather than continuous linear coastlines, its structural exports (available directly in CSV and GeoJSON via Zenodo) provide a rigorous empirical baseline for validating modeled interglacial shorelines.41

### **Web Tile Services for Paleogeography**

Serving monolithic GeoJSON FeatureCollections from a backend API directly to a MapLibre GL frontend is notoriously inefficient for global datasets at high resolutions. When vector nodes number in the hundreds of thousands, this architecture leads to severe browser memory bloat, high payload transfer times, and UI rendering freezes. Modern geospatial architectures solve this bottleneck by abandoning raw GeoJSON payloads in favor of Web Map Tile Services (WMTS) or Vector Tile Services (TMS).  
The EarthByte GPlates Portal currently operates on a sophisticated Cesium WebGL architecture, where high-resolution global digital elevation models are draped with virtual layers.43 For the Paleo Mapper suite, establishing a localized tile service is the industry standard for delivering high-resolution geometry. Open-source solutions such as GeoServer inherently support WMS, WMTS, and WFS protocols.44 By hosting the pre-computed, sub-degree paleo-coastlines within a PostGIS database or directly as optimized shapefiles, GeoServer can generate and serve pbf (Protocolbuffer Binary Format) vector tiles dynamically as the MapLibre client pans and zooms.44  
Alternatively, serverless or lightweight map tile platforms can consume Mapzen tiles or Cloud Optimized GeoTIFF (COG) formats to deliver XYZ tiles natively to the frontend.46 Vector tiles are highly preferable for the Paleo Mapper tool, as they allow MapLibre to handle styling client-side, dynamically rendering mathematically smooth lines irrespective of zoom level, whilst strictly limiting the data payload to the geographic bounding box currently visible on the user's screen.

## **Practical Integration and Data Engineering Pipelines**

The transition from a 1° NetCDF block matrix to smoothly interpolated vector coastlines requires a rigorous reassessment of data size, algorithmic contouring, and geometry simplification. Implementing sub-degree resolution data carelessly will inevitably crash the FastAPI server or the client browser.

### **Data Size vs. Quality Trade-Off**

The shift in spatial resolution carries an exponential data storage and memory processing cost. A global ![][image1] grid contains ![][image8] individual grid cells. A ![][image5] grid (such as the Scotese PaleoDEM) contains ![][image9] grid cells—exactly a 100-fold increase in raw data points.17 Similarly, the 10-minute ICE-7G\_NA grid contains ![][image10] cells.  
While the physical storage size of a 10-minute NetCDF file is relatively small (![][image11] MB per time slice) 5, the true computational bottleneck emerges during vectorization. Extracting an exact ![][image12]\-meter elevation contour (the coastline) from millions of high-resolution cells yields a complex polygon with hundreds of thousands of tightly clustered vertices. If this geometry is serialized and served directly as a raw GeoJSON string, the JSON payload could easily exceed 15–20 MB per time slice. Transferring and parsing a 20 MB JSON file in a browser environment will severely degrade the performance of MapLibre GL. Thus, high-resolution source data *must* be algorithmically simplified before it is serialized.

### **Pre-Computing Static GeoJSON Coastlines**

Extracting contours dynamically on every GET request is computationally prohibitive. Pre-computing the coastlines is vastly superior and absolutely necessary for a fixed array of time slices (e.g., 0–26 ka at 0.5 ka intervals). A robust Python-based data engineering pipeline can be constructed utilizing standard, open-source scientific libraries.

1. **Grid Extraction and Interpolation:** The pipeline begins by parsing the 10-minute NetCDF files using the rasterio or xarray libraries to isolate the topography variable.47 The skimage.measure.find\_contours function (from the scikit-image library) is highly effective for boundary extraction.47 Unlike basic thresholding (which creates blocky pixels), find\_contours utilizes a marching squares algorithm to locate the constant-valued contour (e.g., exactly 0.0 meters for sea level). It linearly interpolates between array values, producing sub-pixel precision and generating mathematically smooth vector lines regardless of the underlying grid's coarseness.49  
2. **Geometry Conversion:** The raw contour coordinate arrays output by skimage are in pixel space. They must be transposed into valid geographic coordinate geometries. This is accomplished using the Shapely library, mapping the pixel bounds back to Latitude/Longitude LineString or Polygon objects utilizing the raster's affine transform matrix.50  
3. **Algorithmic Simplification:** To resolve the massive GeoJSON file size issue, the high-fidelity geometries must be simplified. The Shapely library provides the .simplify(tolerance, preserve\_topology=True) method, which implements the industry-standard Ramer-Douglas-Peucker (RDP) algorithm.51 The tolerance parameter defines the maximum spatial distance a simplified line can deviate from the original geometry.51 By carefully tuning the tolerance parameter (e.g., 0.01 degrees), the pipeline can algorithmically strip thousands of redundant, collinear vertices along relatively straight coastlines while aggressively preserving the high-resolution crenelations of complex bays, estuaries, and peninsulas. This reduction technique can shrink the final GeoJSON payload size by 70–90% without any perceptible loss in visual quality when rendered.53

### **The "Smallest Change" for Dramatic Improvement**

If overhauling the architecture to support GeoServer vector tiles, deploying GPlates Docker containers, or downloading entirely new NetCDF datasets is currently out of scope for the development team, there is a fundamental algorithmic change that will yield dramatically better coastlines with minimal effort.  
The absolute smallest intervention is updating the backend Python script's extraction methodology. Currently, the GET /paleo-coastline endpoint is likely performing a naive grid thresholding operation (e.g., rendering grid cells where elevation \>= 0 as discrete polygon squares, and then attempting to apply an exterior smoothing function). This approach is geometrically flawed.  
By abandoning naive thresholding and introducing skimage.measure.find\_contours (marching squares) and shapely.simplify (RDP) to process the *existing* 1° ICE-6G\_C NetCDF files, the API will output smoothly interpolated vector lines instead of blocky raster representations.49 The marching squares algorithm naturally infers the sub-grid geometry, effectively estimating where the coastline should curve between the 111 km points. While it will not invent missing topobathymetric data, this simple algorithmic shift will instantly transform the MapLibre GL rendering from a jagged, pixelated edge to a fluid, continuous geographic coastline.

## **Strategic Recommendations and Implementation Path**

Based on the exhaustive analysis of available topography models, tectonic rotation APIs, pre-computed datasets, and geospatial engineering techniques, the following multi-tiered implementation path is recommended to systematically upgrade the Paleo Mapper suite.

### **Phase 1: Immediate Term (High Impact, Low Effort)**

**Action:** Replace the 1° ICE-6G\_C (VM5a) data with the 10-minute ICE-7G\_NA (VM7) NetCDF data, and implement the marching squares contouring algorithm in the backend.  
**Reasoning:** The ICE-7G\_NA model offers a near 100-fold increase in raw spatial resolution (reducing cell size to ![][image4] km) while concurrently maintaining the highest geophysical validity for the critical 0–26 ka window.5 Furthermore, the ICE-7G\_NA files are hosted at the exact same University of Toronto repository as the current ICE-6G files, entirely minimizing data acquisition friction and format conversion overhead.5  
**Implementation Sequence:**

1. Download the ICE-7G\_NA 10 min resolution NetCDF archive for the required time slices.5  
2. Modify the FastAPI backend to execute a pre-computation script at server startup, rather than generating data on the fly.  
3. Utilize xarray to load the topobathymetric arrays, apply skimage.measure.find\_contours(topo, 0.0) to extract the exact sea-level boundary 49, and convert these outputs to Shapely polygons.50  
4. Apply geometry.simplify(tolerance=0.01, preserve\_topology=True) to aggressively downsample the vertex count, ensuring the topology remains valid (no self-intersecting lines).51  
5. Write the output to disk as static GeoJSON files, and refactor the FastAPI endpoint to simply serve these optimized static files via the existing API contract.

### **Phase 2: Medium Term (API Modernization and Tectonic Integration)**

**Action:** Deploy a local Docker instance of the GPlates Web Service and transition the MapLibre GL frontend to utilize Vector Tiles.  
**Reasoning:** If the Paleo Mapper suite intends to scale its temporal coverage deeper into the past than 26 ka (e.g., full Quaternary or deep Phanerozoic mapping), standard static grid extraction will fail to account for the lateral movement of tectonic plates. The GPlates Web Service natively handles Euler pole rotations and tectonic tracking, which is mandatory for deep-time accuracy.31 Furthermore, transitioning away from monolithic GeoJSON files to vector tiles is a prerequisite for maintaining application performance as geometric complexity increases.  
**Implementation Sequence:**

1. Execute docker run \-d \--rm \-p 18000:80 gplates/gws to host the GWS natively within the application's backend infrastructure.36 This guarantees sub-millisecond network latency and completely eliminates external dependencies on the public NCI academic server, preventing unexpected throttling.29  
2. Inject the highly accurate Scotese PaleoAtlas v7 shapefiles into the local GPlates database to ensure the rotation engine has access to the best available deep-time coastal geometry.18  
3. For the MapLibre GL frontend, implement a lightweight vector tile server (such as utilizing the tippecanoe utility paired with a static S3 bucket, or deploying a localized GeoServer instance). This infrastructure will dynamically stream the simplified coastal geometries as highly compressed .mvt or .pbf tiles, rendering them on the GPU seamlessly.44

### **Phase 3: Long Term (Cutting-Edge Resolution via Downscaling)**

**Action:** Implement the Anomaly (Delta) Method to fuse low-resolution GIA models with high-resolution modern bathymetry for hyper-local accuracy.  
**Reasoning:** Even at a 10-minute (18.5 km) resolution, regional geomorphology is inherently smoothed. For specialized applications requiring extreme precision—such as determining the viability of localized coastal archaeological sites during the Last Glacial Maximum, or mapping the exact width of paleoriver channels on the exposed Sunda Shelf—an 18.5 km resolution remains insufficient. To achieve true topographical fidelity, the application must adopt the data pipelines utilized by advanced climate models.  
**Implementation Sequence:** Replicate the distributed processing pipeline established by open-source projects like PaleoEurope-DEM.21 Secure a modern, ultra-high-resolution base layer, such as the 15-arc-second ETOPO 2022 dataset or the GEBCO 2023 grid.24 Programmatically subtract the modern ICE-7G\_NA topography prediction from its respective paleo-prediction to isolate the pure GIA and eustatic sea-level anomaly.21 Bilinearly interpolate this low-resolution anomaly grid and mathematically apply it to the 15-arc-second ETOPO 2022 base map.21  
The resulting digital elevation model will boast sub-kilometer precision, capturing precise paleoriver channels, intricate continental shelf inundations, and the exact boundaries of transient land-bridges with unprecedented accuracy. This custom, hyper-resolution dataset can then be contoured using the marching squares algorithm, tiled into .mvt format, and deployed through the Phase 2 tile server. Successfully implementing this downscaling methodology will solidify the Paleo Mapper tool not merely as a visualization aide, but as a premier, state-of-the-art paleogeographic reconstruction suite capable of supporting rigorous academic research.

## **Conclusion**

The current manifestation of angular, blocky coastlines within the Paleo Mapper suite is a dual symptom of both insufficient spatial resolution (the 1° ICE-6G\_C baseline) and rigid, threshold-based scalar-to-vector translation methodologies. While the prompt correctly identified the ICE-6G\_C O512 and the legacy ICE-5G 10-minute models as potential upgrades, a deep geophysical evaluation reveals that they offer either marginal spatial improvements or rely on obsolete mantle viscosity assumptions.  
Instead, adopting the 10-minute ICE-7G\_NA (VM7) model provides an immediate, scientifically rigorous, and highly detailed foundation specifically tailored for the 0–26 ka window.5 For applications extending into deep geological time, the 0.1° Scotese PaleoDEMs and the accompanying PaleoAtlas v7 shapefiles stand as the premier, sea-level adjusted alternatives.17 By fusing these high-resolution data sources with modern Python-based contouring libraries (skimage.measure.find\_contours) and algorithmic vertex simplification (Shapely.simplify), the application can instantly transform its visual fidelity while strictly maintaining client-side computational efficiency.49 Ultimately, evolving the backend architecture to leverage self-hosted GPlates Docker containers and topobathymetric downscaling via the anomaly method will ensure the tool remains highly scalable, geometrically precise, and scientifically authoritative well into the future.21

#### **Works cited**

1. High-Resolution Mass Trends of the Antarctic Ice Sheet through a Spectral Combination of Satellite Gravimetry and Radar Altimetry Observations \- MDPI, accessed May 24, 2026, [https://www.mdpi.com/2072-4292/11/2/144](https://www.mdpi.com/2072-4292/11/2/144)  
2.
